explanation: The sentence says that, in PSV Mode, a new inspiration should start when there is a sudden drop in pressure above the inhale trigger sensitivity (ITS_PSV), which is detected by 'drop(PAW) > ITS_PSV'. The atomic proposition 'dropPAW_greaterThan_ITS_PSV' expresses this event. 'PSVMode' should be active whenever this applies. The requirement is that whenever we are in PSV Mode, if such a drop occurs, then a new inspiration shall be initiated. The 'initiation of inspiration' can be indicated by 'inspiratoryPhaseStart'. The temporal relation is causality: whenever the drop occurs (in PSV mode), eventually a new inspiration phase must start. This is encoded as: Globally, if we are in PSVMode and 'dropPAW_greaterThan_ITS_PSV' occurs, then eventually 'inspiratoryPhaseStart' occurs. Thus, in LTL: G((PSVMode & dropPAW_greaterThan_ITS_PSV) -> F inspiratoryPhaseStart).

explanation:
The aim is to specify, in LTL, that whenever (globally) the PSV mode is active (PSVMode), a new inspiration ('shall be initiated') occurs after a sudden drop in pressure above the inhale trigger sensitivity (dropPAW_greaterThan_ITS_PSV).

First, identify the atomic propositions involved. From the atomic propositions map provided:
- PSVMode: indicates Pressure Support Ventilation Mode is active
- dropPAW_greaterThan_ITS_PSV: a sudden drop in pressure above the inhale trigger sensitivity
- patientBreathingRequest: indicates patient request new breathing cycle (which, in PSV, corresponds to the ventilator initiating a new inspiration upon such request/trigger)

The requirement can be formalized as: whenever PSVMode is true and dropPAW_greaterThan_ITS_PSV occurs, eventually a new inspiration (patientBreathingRequest) must be initiated.

So: globally, if we are in PSVMode and a dropPAW_greaterThan_ITS_PSV is detected, then eventually a patientBreathingRequest will be seen.

Therefore, the LTL specification is: G((PSVMode & dropPAW_greaterThan_ITS_PSV) -> F patientBreathingRequest).

explanation_dict.natural_language: PSV Mode is active
A sudden drop in pressure above inhale trigger sensitivity is detected
A new inspiration is initiated (patient requests new cycle)
If PSVMode and dropPAW_greaterThan_ITS_PSV, then eventually patientBreathingRequest
Globally requirement applies

explanation:
The input sentence describes a triggering condition in PSV (Pressure Support Ventilation) Mode: a new inspiration shall be initiated by (i.e., happens when) a sudden drop in pressure above the inhale trigger sensitivity ITS_PSV, which is modelled by the atomic proposition dropPAW_greaterThan_ITS_PSV. We are not tasked to model all details (e.g., the mode switch), just the core logic: 'Whenever dropPAW_greaterThan_ITS_PSV occurs, a new inspiration is started.' 

This means that globally (G), whenever dropPAW_greaterThan_ITS_PSV is true, it should be immediately, or at the next step, followed by the start of inspiration (inspiratoryPhaseStart). In LTL, the standard pattern for 'whenever p, then eventually q' is G(p -> F q). If it must be the direct consequence (in the next state), we use G(p -> X q). Since the original text says 'shall be initiated by', it is reasonable to use the direct next state for inspiration start. Thus, the formula is: G(dropPAW_greaterThan_ITS_PSV -> X inspiratoryPhaseStart).

explanation: The sentence specifies that, in PSV mode, a new inspiration is initiated when there is a sudden drop in pressure above the inhale trigger sensitivity (ITS_PSV), set by the user. We have atomic propositions: PSVMode (indicates PSV mode active) and dropPAW_greaterThan_ITS_PSV (indicates that the condition drop(PAW) > ITS_PSV is true). The desired property is that whenever, globally, we are in PSVMode, and there is a sudden drop in pressure above ITS_PSV, then at the next step (X), the patient breathing request (patientBreathingRequest) becomes true (i.e., a new breath request initiated by patient). However, if we only focus on the direct input—initiation of new inspiration cycle when drop(PAW) > ITS_PSV in PSVMode—the temporal property becomes: Globally, if we are in PSVMode and if drop(PAW) > ITS_PSV, then in the next step a new breath is requested. So the LTL formula is: G((PSVMode & dropPAW_greaterThan_ITS_PSV) -> X patientBreathingRequest)

explanation:
The natural language specification is: 'PSV Mode: A new inspiration shall be initiated by a sudden drop in pressure above the inhale trigger sensitivity (ITS_PSV), which shall be set by the user (i.e., it yields the condition drop(PAW) > ITS_PSV )).'

First, we identify the key points:
1. 'PSV Mode' indicates the formula applies only when PSVMode holds. This can be translated as PSVMode being true.
2. 'A new inspiration shall be initiated' can be mapped to 'breathingCycleStart', which in this context means the start of a new inspiration.
3. 'A sudden drop in pressure above the inhale trigger sensitivity' is mapped to 'dropPAW_greaterThan_ITS_PSV'.
4. The intended requirement is that whenever PSVMode is active, every time there is a 'breathingCycleStart', it must be caused (or directly preceded) by a 'dropPAW_greaterThan_ITS_PSV'. Typcially in medical context, initiation by a trigger means the trigger event directly causes the event, i.e., the trigger happens at the same time or right before the effect.

In LTL, to capture that each 'breathingCycleStart' (when in PSVMode) is the result of 'dropPAW_greaterThan_ITS_PSV', we assert that globally, whenever we see a transition to 'breathingCycleStart' while in PSVMode, then 'dropPAW_greaterThan_ITS_PSV' held in the previous moment. Since LTL does not have a direct 'previous' operator, a common solution is to say: whenever 'breathingCycleStart' occurs (in PSVMode), it must be immediately preceded by 'dropPAW_greaterThan_ITS_PSV' in the previous state. However, LTL only has the next ('X') operator going forward, so we rewrite it in the following way:

- Globally, if we are in PSVMode, then any time 'dropPAW_greaterThan_ITS_PSV' occurs, it is eventually (more specifically, in the next state or soon) followed by 'breathingCycleStart'.
     OR
- Globally, if we are in PSVMode and 'breathingCycleStart' occurs, then 'dropPAW_greaterThan_ITS_PSV' must have just occurred (which in LTL is approximated as: it's required that 'dropPAW_greaterThan_ITS_PSV' is true at the same time as 'breathingCycleStart' — the best conservative approximation in LTL forward semantics).

Given LTL's future-only semantics, the standard robust encoding is:
    G((PSVMode & breathingCycleStart) -> dropPAW_greaterThan_ITS_PSV)

Alternatively, sometimes people would require:
    G((PSVMode & dropPAW_greaterThan_ITS_PSV) -> F breathingCycleStart)
However, the natural language specification is about 'initiation', which is best approximated with the first formula.

Therefore, the final formula is:
    G((PSVMode & breathingCycleStart) -> dropPAW_greaterThan_ITS_PSV)

explanation:
The requirement is: In PSV Mode, a new inspiration should be initiated by a sudden drop in airway pressure above the user's inhale trigger sensitivity setting (ITS_PSV). This physiological trigger is captured by atomic proposition dropPAW_greaterThan_ITS_PSV. Thus, the LTL property should specify that, whenever the system is in PSV mode (PSVMode), eventually a drop in pressure above the user's threshold (dropPAW_greaterThan_ITS_PSV) will initiate a new inspiration (inspiratoryPhaseStart). This is best expressed as: Globally (for every time step), if we are in PSVMode, whenever dropPAW_greaterThan_ITS_PSV becomes true, eventually inspiratoryPhaseStart will follow.

Thus, we write: G(PSVMode -> (dropPAW_greaterThan_ITS_PSV -> F inspiratoryPhaseStart)). This ensures that, at every moment during PSVMode, if the drop in pressure is detected, eventually a new inspiration will start.

explanation: The sentence specifies a mode in which a new inspiration (breath) is initiated by a sudden drop in airway pressure above a user-set inhale trigger sensitivity (ITS_PSV). This means: whenever such a pressure drop occurs (dropPAW_greaterThan_ITS_PSV is true) and the ventilator is in PSVMode, then eventually the patient's breathing request is recognized (patientBreathingRequest becomes true) and a new inspiratory phase should start (inspiratoryPhaseStart is true). The trigger only applies in PSV mode. The temporal pattern implies: Globally (in PSVMode, if dropPAW_greaterThan_ITS_PSV becomes true, then eventually this yields patientBreathingRequest and inspiratoryPhaseStart). The main LTL pattern becomes: G( (PSVMode & dropPAW_greaterThan_ITS_PSV) -> F (patientBreathingRequest & inspiratoryPhaseStart) ).

explanation: The sentence describes a requirement for PSV Mode: whenever a sudden drop in pressure above the inhale trigger sensitivity (ITS_PSV) is detected, a new inspiration (breathing cycle) should be initiated. In LTL, this means that globally (G), if 'dropPAW_greaterThan_ITS_PSV' holds, then eventually ('F') a 'breathingCycleStart' must occur. This ensures that every triggering pressure drop leads to a new inspiration initiation.

explanation:
The requirement says: In PSV Mode, a new inspiration must be initiated by a sudden drop in pressure above the inhale trigger sensitivity (ITS_PSV), which is user-configured (drop(PAW) > ITS_PSV). We represent this using LTL as follows: 1. 'In PSV Mode' means the condition PSVMode holds globally as a precondition. 2. 'A new inspiration shall be initiated by a sudden drop...' means that whenever a new inspiration is initiated, it must be due to a dropPAW_greaterThan_ITS_PSV. In terms of atomic propositions, if a new inspiration (breathingCycleStart) occurs, it is preceded by dropPAW_greaterThan_ITS_PSV. Therefore, whenever we observe a breathingCycleStart in PSVMode, it must be that, in the previous step, dropPAW_greaterThan_ITS_PSV occurred. This is expressed using the 'next' operator with the relation that whenever dropPAW_greaterThan_ITS_PSV happens, in the next step, breathingCycleStart happens, all under PSVMode. To allow for any number of drops or starts, we'll use 'globally'. Thus: G((PSVMode & X breathingCycleStart) -> dropPAW_greaterThan_ITS_PSV). This means: in every state, if we are in PSVMode and breathingCycleStart is true in the next state, then dropPAW_greaterThan_ITS_PSV is true now (i.e., every new inspiration is due to a pressure drop above the threshold, in PSVMode).

Alternatively, stricter interpretation: Whenever a breath cycle starts in PSVMode, it must have been immediately preceded by dropPAW_greaterThan_ITS_PSV. (That is, in all paths, breathingCycleStart can only happen in the next step after dropPAW_greaterThan_ITS_PSV holds, when in PSVMode.) The formula: G((PSVMode & X(breathingCycleStart)) -> dropPAW_greaterThan_ITS_PSV).

explanation: The core of the requirement is: 'A new inspiration shall be initiated by a sudden drop in pressure above the inhale trigger sensitivity (ITS_PSV).' In the provided atomic propositions, 'dropPAW_greaterThan_ITS_PSV' represents that drop(PAW) > ITS_PSV (the sudden drop). The new inspiration being initiated is mapped to 'patientBreathingRequest': the patient is requesting a new breath, which is the trigger for starting a new inspiration. So the requirement is that whenever 'dropPAW_greaterThan_ITS_PSV' occurs, it leads, possibly after some finite time (since the real system is not instantaneous), to a 'patientBreathingRequest', and this requirement should hold globally (in all states of the system operating in PSV mode). The 'PSVMode' atomic proposition indicates being in PSV mode, so this property is only meaningful when PSV mode holds. Therefore, the translation is: Globally, if we are in PSV mode and there is a sudden drop in pressure ('dropPAW_greaterThan_ITS_PSV'), then eventually ('F') a patient breathing request ('patientBreathingRequest') will be issued (which starts a new inspiration). In LTL: G(PSVMode -> (dropPAW_greaterThan_ITS_PSV -> F patientBreathingRequest))
